text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025

Dr Kate
Health Centre

Keele University, Keele, Newcastle

Staffordshire ST5 5BG
Dear Dr Kate
Wendy Brown, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: K482715 NHS No. 6592841037
Flat 7 Brookside Avenue, Newcastle-under-Lyme, ST5 2QD 

Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset. With a history of Lennox–Gastaut syndrome, previously experiencing tonic seizures, atonic drop attacks and atypical absences, she has now been seizure free for several years. Co-existing migraine without aura.

Neuroimaging/EEG: MRI brain 2018 and 2024 unremarkable for structural epileptogenic lesion. Historical EEGs showed generalised slow spike–wave in childhood; most recent routine EEG (2023) normal background with no interictal epileptiform discharges.

Current medication: Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily; Topiramate 50 mg nightly (for migraine prophylaxis); Folic acid 5 mg daily; Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily.

Not previously tolerated/ceased: Levetiracetam (mood irritability), Valproate (weight gain, discontinued when planning family), Carbamazepine (dizziness), Clobazam PRN previously (no longer required).

Allergies: No known drug allergies.

Interval history: I reviewed Wendy in clinic today; she attended alone and brought her seizure diary. She works in a distribution warehouse and reports that since the peak season ended, overtime and workload have reduced significantly. She links this reduction in fatigue and irregular hours with better overall control and wellbeing. Sleep has improved to 7–8 hours nightly; she has resumed light exercise. With a history of Lennox–Gastaut syndrome, previously experiencing tonic seizures, atonic drop attacks and atypical absences, she has now been seizure free for several years, and in the past 12 months has had no events suggestive of focal aware or impaired awareness seizures. She denies auras, drop attacks, or nocturnal events. No injuries or hospital attendances.

Adverse effects/functional status: No cognitive slowing on current regimen; mood stable; weight stable; no diplopia or ataxia. Migraines have reduced from 4/month to approximately 1/month since starting low-dose topiramate; uses simple analgesia sparingly.

Lifestyle/safety: Adheres to DVLA guidance and remains eligible to drive given sustained seizure freedom. Avoids sleep deprivation and excessive caffeine. No alcohol misuse. Reports good medication adherence with phone reminders.

Plan:
- Continue lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily unchanged.
- Continue topiramate 50 mg nightly for migraine prophylaxis; no change today.
- Routine bloods via GP: FBC, U&E, LFT, vitamin D over next 6–8 weeks for general monitoring.
- Encourage ongoing sleep hygiene and regular routine at work; written information provided on seizure first aid for her employer, though not recently required.
- Follow-up: routine review in 9–12 months, earlier if any breakthrough events, red flags, pregnancy planning, or adverse effects.

Thank you for your ongoing care. Please let me know if there are any concerns in the interim.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: she has now been seizure free for several years
With a history of Lennox–Gastaut syndrome, previously experiencing tonic seizures, atonic drop attacks and atypical absences, she has now been seizure free for several years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient has been seizure free for several years and, in the past 12 months, has had no events suggestive of seizures. This satisfies the criterion of at least 6 months seizure freedom. Therefore, the normalized label should be 'seizure free for multiple years' because the duration is expressed as 'several years' without a precise number.
seizure_frequency_number: she has now been seizure free for several years
reference: With a history of Lennox–Gastaut syndrome, previously experiencing tonic seizures, atonic drop attacks and atypical absences, she has now been seizure free for several years
